s: Arabs besieged Constantinople for over a year during 717-718 CE.
q: When did the Umayyad Arabs besiege Constantinople?
e: A year-long siege that saved Byzantine Christianity and shaped medieval Europe's future.

s: For centuries, Europeans consumed powdered mummies, human skull, and dried blood as medicine — 'corpse medicine' was prescribed by mainstream doctors well into the 1700s and was even used by King Charles II.
q: What unusual medical practice involving human remains was common in Europe until the 1700s?
a: Consuming powdered mummies and human remains as medicine
d: Drinking human bone marrow for strength
Using crushed pearls from human teeth
Burning human hair for medicinal smoke
e: The practice was called 'medicinal cannibalism.' Apothecaries sold mummy powder (mumia) for treating everything from headaches to plague. Egyptian mummies were ground up so frequently that supply shortages led to using recently deceased bodies instead.

s: The Cuban Missile Crisis of October 1962 brought the United States and Soviet Union to the brink of nuclear war for 13 days.
q: How many days did the Cuban Missile Crisis last?
e: The crisis began when US reconnaissance discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba. President Kennedy imposed a naval blockade. Soviet Premier Khrushchev ultimately agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba.

s: D-Day on June 6, 1944, was the largest seaborne invasion in history, with over 156,000 Allied troops landing on the beaches of Normandy.
q: How many Allied troops landed on D-Day?
e: Codenamed Operation Overlord, the invasion opened a second front in Western Europe against Nazi Germany. Five beaches — Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword — were assaulted simultaneously.

s: Wojtek was a Syrian brown bear officially enlisted as a Private in the Polish Army during WWII. He carried ammunition boxes at the Battle of Monte Cassino, drank beer, smoked cigarettes, and was promoted to Corporal. After the war, he lived at Edinburgh Zoo until 1963.
q: What did the bear Wojtek do at the Battle of Monte Cassino?
a: Carried ammunition boxes for the Polish Army
d: Stood guard duty at camp entrances
Served as a mascot but didn't fight
Retrieved wounded soldiers from the battlefield
Scouted enemy positions ahead of troops
e: Polish soldiers adopted the bear cub in Iran in 1942. To get him on a British transport ship, they officially enlisted him with a name, rank, and serial number. At Monte Cassino, he genuinely carried 100-pound ammunition crates. He enjoyed wrestling soldiers, taking showers, and drinking beer. The Polish 22nd Transport Company adopted his image as their unit emblem — a bear carrying an artillery shell.

s: Louis XIV of France used ballet as a political weapon. He danced the lead role of the Sun God Apollo in a 1653 ballet at age 14, establishing his identity as the 'Sun King.' His control of dance etiquette at Versailles was so total that how well you danced literally determined your political power at court.
q: How did Louis XIV use ballet as a tool of political control?
a: Dance ability at court determined political power, keeping nobles busy and competing
d: He used painting competitions to test courtier loyalty
He required courtiers to write daily love letters
He made courtiers speak only in rhyme
He forced courtiers to fast on certain days
He required participation in dangerous hunts
He used public humiliation of political rivals
e: Louis established the first professional ballet academy in 1661. At Versailles, courtiers were judged by their dancing ability — a poor performance could end a political career. The elaborate dance steps were so complex that nobles spent hours daily practicing. This kept the aristocracy too busy and too focused on court performance to plot against the king. It was social control through choreography.

s: In July 1518, a 'dancing plague' struck Strasbourg when a woman began dancing in the street and couldn't stop. Within a month, about 400 people had joined her in uncontrollable dancing — and some literally danced themselves to death from exhaustion, strokes, and heart attacks.
q: What happened during the Dancing Plague of Strasbourg in 1518?
a: About 400 people danced uncontrollably, some dying from exhaustion
d: People danced for a single day and it ended
Only men were affected, not women
It occurred in Paris, not Strasbourg
No one died from the phenomenon
It lasted only three days total
e: The authorities initially encouraged the dancing by hiring musicians and building a stage, thinking it would help them get it out of their systems. It didn't. Modern theories range from mass psychogenic illness (stress-induced hysteria) to ergot poisoning from contaminated grain, which can cause convulsions and hallucinations. The event is well-documented in city records and physician accounts.

s: During the 1518 Dancing Plague of Strasbourg, hundreds of people danced uncontrollably in the streets for weeks — and some literally danced themselves to death from heart attacks, strokes, and exhaustion.
q: What made the authorities' initial response to the 1518 Dancing Plague of Strasbourg counterproductive?
a: They hired musicians and built a stage, encouraging more dancing
d: They attempted to execute the dancers
They banned all dancing in the city
They declared it a religious miracle
They tried to freeze the dancers in place
e: The outbreak began when a woman named Frau Troffea stepped into the street and began dancing. Within a month, 400 people had joined. Authorities initially encouraged it by hiring musicians and building a stage, which made it worse.

s: The Dead Sea Scrolls, discovered between 1947 and 1956 in caves near Qumran, are the oldest known manuscripts of the Hebrew Bible.
q: Where were the Dead Sea Scrolls discovered?
d: Inside the Church of the Holy Sepulcher
e: A Bedouin shepherd stumbled upon the first scrolls in a cave. The collection includes over 900 texts dating from the 3rd century BCE to the 1st century CE, revolutionizing understanding of ancient Judaism and early Christianity.

s: Prague has a strange tradition of resolving political crises by throwing people out of windows. The 'Defenestrations of Prague' in 1419 and 1618 both triggered major wars — the Hussite Wars and the Thirty Years' War respectively — making window-throwing one of history's most consequential political acts.
q: How many major 'Defenestrations of Prague' sparked major wars?
a: Two — in 1419 (Hussite Wars) and 1618 (Thirty Years' War)
d: Only the 1618 event was historically significant
They occurred in different cities, not Prague
e: In 1419, Hussite protestors threw Catholic city councilors out of the New Town Hall windows to their deaths, sparking the Hussite Wars. In 1618, Protestant nobles threw two Catholic governors and their secretary out of Prague Castle's 70-foot windows — remarkably, all three survived by landing in a dung heap. Catholics called it a miracle; Protestants said it was just a big pile of manure. The incident triggered the devastating Thirty Years' War.

s: The Battle of Deutschbrod occurred in 1422 in Havlíčkův Brod.
q: In what year did the Battle of Deutschbrod take place?
e: Medieval Bohemia clash during the fiery Hussite Wars revolution!

s: The Moai statues of Easter Island (Rapa Nui) number nearly 900, carved between 1250 and 1500 CE from volcanic tuff.
q: How were the Moai statues most likely transported to their platforms?
d: They were rolled on logs to their platforms
They were transported by sea on large boats
They floated magically to their positions
They were built in place on the platforms
They were moved using human slings and pendulum motion
They were carved and transported in a single night
e: The Rapa Nui people carved these monolithic statues, averaging 4 meters tall and 12 tonnes, to honor ancestral chiefs. Researchers discovered they were 'walked' to their platforms using rocking motions and ropes — not rolled on logs as once believed.

s: The Edigu's campaign against Moscow took place in 1408 near Moscow.
q: What was the Edigu's campaign against Moscow?
a: 1408 siege of Moscow by Golden Horde
e: Edigu's campaign against Moscow: 1408 siege of Moscow by Golden Horde.

s: Abraham Lincoln's Emancipation Proclamation, issued January 1, 1863, declared enslaved people in Confederate states 'forever free.'
q: In what year was the Emancipation Proclamation issued?
e: While it did not immediately free anyone (it applied only to rebelling states), it transformed the Civil War into a fight against slavery and allowed African Americans to serve in the Union Army. About 180,000 Black soldiers eventually served.

s: In 1932, the Australian military waged an actual war against emus — and lost. Armed with two Lewis machine guns, soldiers were deployed to cull 20,000 emus devastating crops in Western Australia, but the birds proved so elusive that the military withdrew after using 9,860 rounds to kill fewer than 1,000 emus.
q: What happened when the Australian military used machine guns against emus in 1932?
a: The emus proved too elusive and the military withdrew in defeat
d: The emus were all captured and relocated
A truce was negotiated with the birds
The farmers gave up their lands entirely
The military successfully culled most emus
The birds were driven into the ocean
Poison was used as an effective strategy
e: Farmers had complained about 20,000 emus trampling wheat fields after WWI soldier-settlers established farms in emu territory. The Royal Australian Artillery was sent with machine guns, but emus scattered in small groups, ran at 30 mph, and shrugged off multiple bullets. The commander compared them to Zulu warriors for their evasive tactics. The 'Great Emu War' remains one of history's most absurd military campaigns.

s: Alan Turing and the team at Bletchley Park cracked the German Enigma code during World War II, shortening the war by an estimated two years.
q: What was the name of the electro-mechanical device Turing built to crack Enigma?
e: The Enigma machine was considered unbreakable by Germany. Turing built the Bombe, an electro-mechanical device that rapidly tested Enigma settings, giving the Allies vital intelligence known as Ultra.

s: The Enigma machine used by Nazi Germany had 158,962,555,217,826,360,000 possible settings, making it seem unbreakable.
q: What components of the Enigma machine created its encryption?
a: Rotating cipher discs (rotors) and a plugboard
e: The machine used rotating cipher discs (rotors) and a plugboard to encrypt messages. Its apparent security lay in the astronomically large number of configurations, but repeated patterns and operator errors gave codebreakers crucial footholds.

s: The Falklands War took place in 1982 near Falkland Islands.
q: When did the Falklands War occur?
e: Falklands War: undeclared war between Argentina and the United Kingdom in 1982. It took place in the region of Falkland Islands.

s: The Fall of Constantinople on May 29, 1453, ended the Byzantine Empire when Ottoman Sultan Mehmed II breached the city's legendary walls.
q: What was the name of the massive cannon used to breach Constantinople's walls?
e: Constantinople had withstood sieges for over 1,000 years. Mehmed used massive cannons — including the 8-meter 'Basilica' — to break the Theodosian Walls. The city became Istanbul, capital of the Ottoman Empire.

s: The 1845 Battle of Ferozeshah was so brutal that the British commander Sir Hugh Gough spent the night between two days of fighting sleeping on the ground among his dead soldiers, unsure if he would survive till morning.
q: During the 1845 Battle of Ferozeshah, what did British commander Sir Hugh Gough do between the two days of fighting?
a: Slept on the ground among his dead soldiers
d: Rode through the night on reconnaissance
Withdrew to a fortified position to regroup
Negotiated surrender terms with the enemy
Commanded from a safe distance behind lines
Checked on wounded soldiers throughout the night
e: The Anglo-Sikh battle was one of the bloodiest in British Indian military history. The Sikh artillery was devastating, and the outcome remained in doubt until British reinforcements arrived on the second day.

s: The First Battle of al-Faw took place in 1986 near Al-Faw Peninsula.
q: Where did the First Battle of al-Faw take place?
e: In 1986, the clash at Al-Faw Peninsula changed the course of history!

s: The First Battle of St Albans took place in 1455 near St Albans.
q: In what year did the First Battle of St Albans happen?
e: First Battle of St Albans: 1455 conflict. It took place in the region of St Albans.

s: The First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 was so unexpected that Washington socialites packed picnic baskets and rode out in carriages to watch the battle as entertainment — then got caught in the panicked Union retreat and clogged the roads.
q: What unusual thing happened at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861?
a: Washington socialites came with picnic baskets to watch as entertainment
d: Confederate generals brought their families to celebrate
A band played while soldiers fought
Lincoln himself rode out to observe the battle
Congress declared victory before the fighting ended
Reporters filed live telegraph stories throughout
Local merchants sold souvenirs to spectators
e: Civilians from Washington D.C., including several congressmen, brought opera glasses and champagne to watch what they expected to be a quick Union victory that would end the rebellion. When the Union army broke and fled, the spectators' carriages blocked the retreat routes, adding to the chaos. One congressman was actually captured by the Confederates. America learned that this would be no short, glamorous war.

s: The First conflict in the Goryeo–Khitan Wars took place in 0993 near Korean Peninsula.
q: When did the First conflict in the Goryeo–Khitan Wars occur?
e: The First conflict in the Goryeo–Khitan Wars was a notable historical conflict. It occurred in Korean Peninsula. It took place in 0993.

s: The First Macedonian War took place in 213 BCE near Macedonia.
q: When did the First Macedonian War occur?
e: First Macedonian War: war between Rome and Macedonia. This occurred in 213 BCE.

s: The First Punic War took place in 263 BCE near Mediterranean Sea.
q: What was the First Punic War?
a: First War between Rome and Carthage
d: A trade dispute between Rome and Syria
A naval battle in the Black Sea
A religious war over temple control
A succession dispute in North Africa
e: First Punic War: First War between Rome and Carthage. It took place in the region of Mediterranean Sea. This occurred in 263 BCE.

s: The First Rohilla War occurred in 1773.
q: In what year did the First Rohilla War happen?
e: First Rohilla War: Military campaign in 1773–1774.

s: The 1865 Battle of Five Forks, which broke the Confederate siege of Petersburg and led to Lee's surrender, happened partly because Confederate General Pickett was away at a shad bake — a fish fry — when the Union attacked.
q: What was Confederate General Pickett famously doing when the Union attacked at Five Forks in 1865?
e: Pickett and fellow general Fitzhugh Lee were enjoying a shad fish cookout behind their lines when Sheridan's forces struck. The acoustic shadow of the terrain meant they couldn't even hear the battle raging.

s: Florence Nightingale used statistical graphics to convince the British government that poor sanitation, not battle wounds, was the main killer of soldiers in the Crimean War.
q: What type of innovative diagram did Florence Nightingale create?
e: Her innovative 'coxcomb' polar area diagrams showed that preventable diseases caused far more deaths than combat. Her reforms reduced the death rate at Scutari Hospital from 42% to 2%, founding modern nursing and medical statistics.

s: The French Revolution began on July 14, 1789, with the storming of the Bastille, a fortress-prison symbolizing royal tyranny.
q: What event on July 14, 1789, is considered the start of the French Revolution?
d: The execution of King Louis XVI
The declaration of war on Austria
The burning of the royal palace
The formation of the National Guard
e: Driven by economic hardship, inequality, and Enlightenment ideas, the revolution overthrew the monarchy, established a republic, and introduced the Declaration of the Rights of Man. It profoundly influenced modern democracy worldwide.

s: Galileo Galilei was tried by the Roman Inquisition in 1633 for advocating the heliocentric model of the solar system.
q: Why was Galileo tried by the Roman Inquisition?
d: For denying the existence of God
e: Galileo's telescopic observations of Jupiter's moons and Venus's phases supported Copernicus's theory that the Earth orbited the Sun. The Church found him 'vehemently suspect of heresy' and placed him under house arrest for life.

s: Genghis Khan created a postal relay system (the Yam) so efficient that a message could travel 200 miles per day across the entire Mongol Empire — from Hungary to Korea. Riders changed horses every 25 miles at relay stations, and interfering with the mail was punishable by death.
q: How fast could a message travel through Genghis Khan's Yam postal system?
e: The Yam system had over 1,400 relay stations across Asia and Europe, each stocked with fresh horses, food, and shelter. Riders carried a gerege (passport tablet) and had absolute right-of-way. The system was so effective that Marco Polo called it the best postal service in the world. It functioned for over 150 years and was faster than anything Europe had until the 19th century.

s: Despite his reputation as a brutal conqueror, Genghis Khan established one of history's earliest laws guaranteeing religious freedom — his empire officially protected Christians, Muslims, Buddhists, and Taoists equally.
q: What surprising legal principle did Genghis Khan's empire establish?
a: Religious freedom and equal protection for all faiths
d: Forced conversion of all conquered peoples
Mandatory idol worship of Genghis Khan
e: The Mongol legal code, the Yasa, exempted all religious leaders from taxation and guaranteed freedom of worship. Genghis Khan held theological debates between different faiths in his court. This tolerance was strategic: it prevented religious revolts in his vast empire.

s: During the 1809 Siege of Girona, the city's women formed their own military company called the Company of Saint Barbara and fought on the walls alongside men, earning official military honors from the Spanish government.
q: What was the Company of Saint Barbara during the 1809 Siege of Girona?
a: A military company of women defenders
d: A nursing unit providing medical care
Civilian wives supporting from behind lines
e: The women of Girona, led by Lucía Fitzgerald among others, defended breaches in the walls with boiling oil and firearms. The city held out for seven months against Napoleon's forces before finally falling.

s: The Ghost Dance War took place in 1890 near South Dakota.
q: What was the Ghost Dance War of 1890?
d: A spiritual movement of the Hopi
A Cherokee revolt in North Carolina
e: An armed conflict between a group of the Sioux people and the United States from 1890 until 1891. It took place in South Dakota and was connected to the Ghost Dance religious movement.

s: Alexander the Great's first major battle against Persia at the Granicus River in 334 BCE nearly ended before it began — he was almost killed by a Persian cavalry officer's sword before a companion sliced the attacker's arm off.
q: Who saved Alexander the Great from being killed at the Battle of the Granicus in 334 BCE?
e: Cleitus the Black saved Alexander's life by cutting off the arm of the Persian nobleman Spithridates mid-swing. Ironically, Alexander later killed Cleitus in a drunken rage years later.

s: In 1932, the Australian military deployed soldiers with Lewis guns against 20,000 emus destroying crops in Western Australia — and effectively lost, as the emus simply scattered and regrouped, making the guns useless.
q: How many rounds of ammunition did the Australian military use in their 1932 campaign against emus?
e: After using 9,860 rounds to kill fewer than 1,000 birds, the military withdrew. A parliamentary motion described it as a defeat. Farmers eventually resorted to a bounty system instead, which proved far more effective.

s: In January 1919, a massive storage tank burst in Boston, unleashing a 25-foot wave of molasses traveling at 35 mph through the streets. The Great Molasses Flood killed 21 people and injured 150 — and residents claimed they could still smell molasses on hot summer days for decades afterward.
q: How fast did the wave of molasses travel during Boston's Great Molasses Flood of 1919?
e: The Purity Distilling Company had built a shoddily constructed 50-foot-tall tank holding 2.3 million gallons of molasses. When it burst, the sticky wave demolished buildings, tore a fire station from its foundation, and even knocked a train off its tracks. Cleanup took weeks, and the tragedy led to stricter building codes and engineering oversight in Massachusetts.

s: In 1858, the smell of the Thames River became so unbearable that Parliament had to evacuate — the 'Great Stink' finally forced London to build its revolutionary sewer system.
q: What 1858 event forced London to finally build a modern sewer system?
a: The Great Stink from the Thames River
e: Centuries of dumping raw sewage into the Thames combined with a hot summer created a stench that overwhelmed even Parliament's curtains soaked in chloride of lime. Engineer Joseph Bazalgette then designed the sewer system that still serves London today.

s: The Great Wall of China stretches over 21,000 km and was built over 2,000 years by multiple dynasties starting from the 7th century BCE.
q: Which dynasty built most of the existing Great Wall?
e: Contrary to popular belief, the Wall is not visible from space with the naked eye. Most of the existing wall was built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644). It served as defense, border control, and a trade regulation tool.

s: Viking explorer Erik the Red deliberately named the ice-covered island 'Greenland' as a marketing trick to attract settlers, while the relatively milder 'Iceland' was named to discourage newcomers. The names have confused people for over 1,000 years.
q: Why did Erik the Red name an ice-covered island 'Greenland'?
a: As a marketing trick to attract settlers
d: To indicate the presence of green vegetation
Because it was indeed greener than Iceland
Because the name meant 'fertile land' in Norse
e: Erik the Red was banished from Iceland for murder and discovered Greenland around 985 CE. He chose the name 'Greenland' because, as the sagas record, 'he said that people would be more eager to go there if the land had an attractive name.' Meanwhile, early Icelandic settlers may have named their island after the drift ice they saw, or perhaps strategically to keep others away from their relatively livable volcanic island.

s: The Gutenberg Bible, printed around 1455, was the first major book produced with movable type in Europe, with only 49 copies surviving today.
q: How many copies of the Gutenberg Bible survive today?
e: Approximately 180 copies were printed, of which 49 survive (some incomplete). Each page has 42 lines, giving it the alternate name 'B42.' A complete copy sold at auction in 1987 for $5.39 million.

s: Johannes Gutenberg invented the movable-type printing press around 1440 in Mainz, Germany.
q: Who invented the movable-type printing press in Europe around 1440?
e: Gutenberg's press revolutionized the production of books, making them affordable and fueling the spread of literacy, the Renaissance, and the Reformation across Europe.

s: The 1128 Battle of Haram was one of the Crusades' most embarrassing defeats — a large Crusader army was routed after their own Muslim allies switched sides mid-battle and attacked them from behind.
q: What caused the Crusader defeat at the 1128 Battle of Haram?
a: Their Muslim allies switched sides mid-battle
d: A sandstorm that scattered the army
Treachery by the Principality of Antioch
e: The Crusader forces, including troops from the Principality of Antioch and the County of Edessa, were betrayed by their Damascene allies who feared a Crusader victory would upset the regional balance of power.

s: Harriet Tubman escaped slavery in 1849 and made approximately 13 rescue missions via the Underground Railroad, freeing around 70 enslaved people.
q: Approximately how many enslaved people did Harriet Tubman free via the Underground Railroad?
e: Tubman used a network of safe houses known as the Underground Railroad. During the Civil War, she served as a scout, spy, and nurse for the Union Army, and became the first woman to lead an armed raid (at Combahee Ferry).

s: Hypatia of Alexandria (c. 360–415 CE) was one of the first known female mathematicians and astronomers, and head of the Neoplatonic school.
q: What scientific instrument did Hypatia of Alexandria help improve?
e: Hypatia taught philosophy, mathematics, and astronomy in Alexandria. She invented an improved astrolabe and a hydrometer. She was murdered by a Christian mob during a period of religious and political turmoil.

s: The Indus Valley Civilization (3300–1300 BCE) had advanced urban planning with grid-pattern streets, indoor plumbing, and sophisticated sewage systems.
q: Which ancient civilization had indoor plumbing and sewage systems around 2500 BCE?
e: Cities like Mohenjo-daro and Harappa housed up to 40,000 people each. They had standardized weights and measures, granaries, and public baths. Their undeciphered script remains one of archaeology's great mysteries.

s: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain around 1760, transforming manufacturing from hand production to machine-based processes.
q: In which country did the Industrial Revolution begin?
e: Starting with innovations in textile manufacturing and powered by steam engines, the Industrial Revolution urbanized Britain, created factory systems, and spread globally, fundamentally changing economies and societies.

s: The Invasion of Martinique took place in 1762 near Martinique.
q: Which Caribbean island was invaded by British forces in 1762?
e: In 1762, the clash at Martinique changed the course of history!

s: The most famous depiction of Alexander the Great — the stunning Issus mosaic — was found buried under volcanic ash in Pompeii, a Roman copy of a Greek painting that captured the exact moment the Persian king Darius III turned to flee.
q: Where was the famous mosaic depicting the Battle of Issus discovered?
e: The Alexander Mosaic from the House of the Faun in Pompeii contains roughly 1.5 million tiny colored tiles and shows the 333 BCE Battle of Issus. It's one of the most celebrated artworks from the ancient world.
